topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The laptop screen glowed with an almost unnerving intensity in the dim living room. I was making tea, the clinking of the ceramic mug against the saucer the loudest sound in the apartment, when I noticed it. A folder, titled simply "Project: Echoes," sat open on Mark’s desktop. My breath hitched. My fingers, still holding the teapot, began to tremble, a slight, involuntary vibration running up my arm. I crept closer, my steps unnaturally quiet on the hardwood floor. The words blurred before my eyes—descriptions of my morning routine, the way I laughed when I was distracted, the specific shade of blue in my eyes. A cold wash spread through my chest, tightening my throat until I couldn't swallow. When Mark walked in, whistling a cheerful tune, I quickly slammed the laptop shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the sudden silence. "Just... working late," I managed, my voice sounding thin and reedy. His smile didn't quite reach his eyes, and that was all the confirmation I needed.

[story 2]

I found the manuscript tucked inside a book I hadn't seen before, hidden beneath a stack of old philosophy texts. The paper felt brittle under my fingertips, and the ink seemed to swim before my gaze. It was undeniably about me. Every anecdote, every unspoken vulnerability I’d ever shared, was meticulously rendered in prose. A sudden, sharp intake of air stole my ability to move. My hands started to sweat, slicking my palms as I tried to pull the pages away, but they felt stuck, tethered to the narrative. I backed away slowly, bumping against the bookshelf. My heart hammered against my ribs with such force I thought it might crack a bone. When I looked up, Mark was standing in the doorway, his expression unreadable, yet somehow intensely focused. The air in the room felt suddenly too thin to breathe.

[story 3]

It started as a casual browsing session, clicking through shared documents, and then I found it. A sprawling narrative, intimate and devastatingly accurate. My skin prickled, a clammy sensation rising from my fingertips to my scalp. I stared at the paragraphs detailing my insecurities, the precise cadence of my arguments with him, the secret hopes I’d never voiced aloud. A wave of intense disorientation washed over me. I pressed my palms against my eyes, trying to physically block out the sight, but the words seemed imprinted behind my eyelids. I scrambled backward until my back hit the cool plaster of the wall. Every small sound—the distant traffic, the hum of the refrigerator—seemed amplified, menacing. When Mark approached, his shadow falling over me, I couldn't form a coherent sentence. I just stared at the floor, my entire body rigid, waiting for the inevitable shift in the atmosphere.
